phase1.explanation:
LinkedList implements both List and Deque interfaces:

Key operations:

As List:
• add(element) — adds to end
• add(index, element) — inserts at position
• get(index) — gets at position
• remove(index) — removes at position

As Deque/Queue:
• addFirst() / addLast() — adds at beginning/end
• getFirst() / getLast() — retrieves without removing
• removeFirst() / removeLast() — removes and returns
• peek() / peekFirst() / peekLast() — looks without removing
• poll() / pollFirst() / pollLast() — removes and returns (null if empty)

As Stack:
• push() — adds to front (LIFO)
• pop() — removes from front

Performance:
• get(index): O(n) — slower than ArrayList
• addFirst: O(1) — faster than ArrayList
• removeFirst: O(1) — faster than ArrayList

ArrayList vs LinkedList:
• ArrayList: fast random access, slow insertion/deletion at front
• LinkedList: slow random access, fast insertion/deletion at front

When to use LinkedList:
• Frequent insertion/removal at beginning or end
• Implementing stacks or queues
• When you don't need random access

When to use ArrayList:
• Frequent random access by index
• More memory efficient (less overhead)

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates a LinkedList<Integer>
2. Adds numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 using addLast() or add()
3. Uses addFirst() to add 0 at the beginning
4. Uses addLast() to add 6 at the end
5. Prints the list
6. Removes the first element using removeFirst()
7. Removes the last element using removeLast()
8. Prints the list again
9. Uses push() and pop() to demonstrate stack behavior
dialogueHints: Use add() or addLast() for initial numbers
addFirst(0) adds to beginning, addLast(6) adds to end
push() adds to front, pop() removes from front
